figures.caption: Fig. 2: Control profiles, shown from top to bottom: adjoint starting from scratch, direct PINN, indirect PINN, and adjoint initialized from the direct PINN solution, which is treated as the reference value for this optimal control problem. The relative L2 errors of control are shown in titles. Note that according to the optimality condition, the terminal solution is y(T ) = −βQu(T ), βQ = 1× 10−3.

Fig. 1: Loss histories for the three approaches: adjoint optimization starting from scratch (top), direct PINN (middle), and indirect PINN (bottom). In the adjoint panel, the legend shows the total objective J , the terminal tracking term JT , the control regularization term Ju, and the relative L2 error of the control, relL2(u). In the direct PINN panel, the legend additionally separates the PDE, boundary-condition (BC), and initial-condition (IC) residual losses, together with JT , Ju, and relL2(u). In the indirect PINN panel, the legend further includes the residual losses associated with the adjoint equation (PDE λ), adjoint boundary condition (BC λ), and terminal optimality condition (T (λ)), in addition to the state PDE/BC/IC residuals, the total loss, and relL2(u). For the PINN cases, only the SSBroyden phase is shown after the short Adam warm-up, and the total wall-clock time of each method is indicated in the panel title.

Fig. 3: State trajectories corresponding to the converged controls, shown from top to bottom: adjoint starting from scratch, direct PINN, indirect PINN, and adjoint initialized from the direct PINN solution. For the PINN-based methods, each panel also compares the state predicted directly by the neural network with the state recomputed by the numerical solver using the converged PINN control. The relative L2 error of each time snapshot is marked in the legend.
